feedback.text: The digital fairness act touches upon some adjacent points to the same issues the recently closed with 1400000+ (count yet to be verified, but very likely to have passed due to the incredible margin). Those issues being that it's common practice in the video game industry (and frankly, software as a whole), to have single point of payment for "perpetual" access, for all intents and purposes a purchase, while still having the game/software reliant on the centralized, company owned, servers. Which will, and in many cases have already been, shut down at some undisclosed point in the future, essentially rendering what at the time of transaction very much look and felt like a purchase, unusable and unplayable. I think it's a good fit to include something in the digital fairness act that if the transaction is akin to a purchase of a product, while still reliant on a company server, there should be some end of life plan, once the company no longer wishes to, or can, support it. When I pay a fee every month to watch movies on a streaming service, like Netflix, it feels like a subscription. The library and movies aren't "mine", I temporarily gain access to them in a rental fashion. When I pay upwards of 50 for a copy of "The Crew", a game by multi billion euro revenue publisher Ubisoft in 2016, and then suddenly cannot play that game in a random unpredictable date in 2024, 8 years later. What at the time felt like a purchase is suddenly revoked. With no way for me to play the game so much as alone, let alone connect directly to friends and play with them without support from Ubisoft. They claim in their EULA that it's a "license" and the shutdown of servers, and subsequent disabling of the ability to play the game, is something that license gives them permission to do. But I think that this is an unfair, consumer hostile, very disingenuous and just plain misleading. This is why I think addressing this issue in the Digital Fairness Act would make a lot of sense and be in the spirit of what the act is about in general!
